definition: The feeling of deep affection, compassion, and empathy toward others. Aroha is central to Māori values and relationships.

example_sentences.maori: He aroha whakatō, he aroha puta mai.
example_sentences.english: If you sow love, love will be returned.

definition: Extended family group. Also a verb meaning to be born or to give birth. Whānau is the primary social unit in Māori society.
Food; also used as a verb meaning to eat. Kai is central to Māori hospitality and manaakitanga.
The open sea, ocean, or a large body of water. The moana is deeply significant in Māori culture as an ancestral pathway and source of sustenance.

example_sentences.maori: Ka hoe ia ki te moana.
example_sentences.english: He paddled out to the sea.

definition: A mountain or high peak. Maunga are often the ancestor mountains of iwi and central to tribal identity.
A river, stream, or channel. Rivers are ancestral connectors and named as tīpuna (ancestors) by many iwi.

example_sentences.maori: Ka rere te awa ki te moana.
example_sentences.english: The river flows to the sea.

definition: Forest or bush. The ngahere is a taonga (treasure) — a rich ecosystem and source of rongoā (medicine), food, and building materials.

example_sentences.maori: He taonga nui te ngahere ki a mātou.
example_sentences.english: The forest is a great treasure to us.

definition: Literally 'a standing place for the feet' — the place where one belongs, where one has the right to stand and speak. Usually the land of one's iwi or hapū.

example_sentences.english: Waikato is my place to stand.

definition: The practice of showing respect, generosity, and care for others — especially guests. A core Māori value expressed through hospitality and service.

example_sentences.maori: Ko te manaakitanga te mea nui.
example_sentences.english: Hospitality is the most important thing.

definition: The responsibility to guard, protect, and care for the natural world. Kaitiakitanga is the obligation passed down from the atua (gods) to protect the taiao (natural environment).

example_sentences.maori: He kaitiaki tātou o te taiao.
example_sentences.english: We are guardians of the natural world.

definition: Genealogy; the layering of one upon another. Whakapapa links people to their ancestors, the natural world, and the atua. It is the framework through which all knowledge is organised.

example_sentences.maori: Ko te whakapapa te ara ki ōu tīpuna.
example_sentences.english: Genealogy is the pathway to your ancestors.

definition: A canoe or vessel. Waka were the vessels that carried Māori ancestors across the Pacific to Aotearoa. Today waka ama (outrigger canoes) remain an important taonga.

example_sentences.maori: Ka hoe tātou i runga i te waka.
example_sentences.english: We will paddle together in the canoe.

definition: A paddle or oar; also the verb meaning to paddle. Hoe is fundamental to waka ama — the paddlers call 'hoe!' to synchronise strokes.
The float or outrigger attached to the side of a waka ama. The ama provides stability on the water.

example_sentences.maori: Kei raro te ama i te wai.
example_sentences.english: The outrigger float is in the water.

definition: The nose or the bow (front) of a canoe. The ihu waka (prow) is often carved with intricate designs representing the waka's identity.

example_sentences.maori: Ko te ihu o te waka e aro ana ki te rā.
example_sentences.english: The prow of the canoe faces the sun.

definition: A versatile greeting meaning 'be well' or 'hello'. Also used to express thanks or acknowledgement. The most widely used Māori phrase in Aotearoa New Zealand.

example_sentences.maori: Kia ora! Kei te pēhea koe?

definition: A formal greeting directed at one person. More respectful than kia ora, typically used in formal or ceremonial contexts. Tēnā kōrua (two people), tēnā koutou (three or more).
A common greeting asking how someone is. The response is typically 'kei te pai' (I'm well/good) or 'kei te māuiui' (I'm unwell).

example_sentences.maori: Kei te pēhea koe? — Kei te pai, ā koe?
example_sentences.english: How are you? — I'm well, and you?

definition: An expression of approval or praise. Can be used to mean 'good', 'well done', 'excellent', or 'that's fine'. Widely used across Aotearoa.
The number one. Also used to mean 'together' or 'as one' in phrases like 'kotahi tātou' (we are one).
The number two. Also refers to a pit or storage house (rua kūmara — sweet potato storage pit).
The number three.
The number four.
The number five. Also the word for hand, as a hand has five fingers.
The number six.

example_sentences.maori: E ono ngā rā o te wiki.
example_sentences.english: There are six days of the week (counting work days).

definition: The number seven.

example_sentences.maori: E whitu ngā rā o te wiki.
example_sentences.english: There are seven days in a week.

definition: The number eight.

example_sentences.maori: E waru ngā tāne i tō mātou kapa haka.
example_sentences.english: There are eight men in our kapa haka group.

definition: The number nine.
The number ten. Forms the basis of higher numbers: tekau mā tahi (eleven), tekau mā rua (twelve), etc.

example_sentences.maori: E tekau ngā tamariki i te akomanga.
example_sentences.english: There are ten children in the classroom.

definition: White; also meaning clean or pure.
Black or dark in colour. Also pango or kōura.
Red. Associated with blood, the rising sun, and vitality.

example_sentences.maori: He whero te rara o te kākahu.
example_sentences.english: The stripe on the garment is red.

definition: Green in colour; also the name for the native green parakeet of Aotearoa. The colour takes its name from the bird.

example_sentences.maori: He kākāriki ngā rau o te rākau.
example_sentences.english: The leaves of the tree are green.

definition: Blue. The colour of the sky (rangi) reflected in water.

example_sentences.english: The sky tomorrow will be blue.

definition: Mother; mum. One of the first words children learn.
Father; dad. Not to be confused with Papa (earth, as in Papatūānuku — the earth mother).
Children (plural). Singular is tamāhine (girl/daughter) or tama (boy/son). Tamariki are cherished in Māori society as the future of the iwi.

example_sentences.maori: Ko ngā tamariki te iho o tō tātou ao.
example_sentences.english: Children are the essence of our world.

definition: An elderly woman or female elder. Kuia hold a place of great respect in Māori communities as keepers of knowledge, tradition, and waiata.

example_sentences.maori: Ka kōrero te kuia ki ngā tamariki.
example_sentences.english: The elderly woman spoke to the children.

definition: An elderly man or male elder. Koroua are respected for their wisdom and knowledge of tradition and tikanga.
Land or earth; also the placenta. The connection between whenua (land) and whenua (placenta) represents the deep bond between people and their land — both nurture life.

example_sentences.maori: E hoki ana au ki tōku whenua.
example_sentences.english: I am returning to my land.

definition: The sky or heavens. Ranginui (the sky father) is one of the principal atua (gods) in Māori cosmology. Also means 'day' as in 'he aha tēnei rangi?' (what day is this?).

example_sentences.maori: Ka nui ngā whetū o te rangi.
example_sentences.english: The stars of the sky are many.

definition: The sun; also 'day'. The rā is the source of warmth and light, and features prominently in karakia (prayer) and waiata (song).

example_sentences.maori: Ka ura te rā i tēnei ata.

definition: The moon; a month (measured by the lunar cycle); also means to understand or to be clear. The Māori lunar calendar (maramataka) was used to guide planting, fishing, and other activities.

example_sentences.maori: He ātaahua te marama i tēnei pō.

definition: Water; also the question word 'who' (ko wai? — who is it?). Wai is precious and sacred in Māori culture.

Prestige, authority, and spiritual power. Mana can be inherited (mana tūpuna), earned (mana tangata), or given (mana tūāhu). Protecting and enhancing the mana of others is central to Māori social interaction.

example_sentences.maori: Me tiaki i te mana o ōu tīpuna.
example_sentences.english: Protect the mana of your ancestors.

definition: Sacred, under spiritual restriction, or forbidden. Tapu protects people, places, and objects that are spiritually significant. The word 'taboo' in English is derived from tapu.
Free from tapu or restriction; ordinary, safe, and accessible. Noa is the complement of tapu — together they govern the spiritual status of people, places, and things.

example_sentences.english: This place is now free from restriction.

definition: A prayer, incantation, or ritual chant. Karakia are used to open and close meetings, before meals, and at the start of any significant activity to invite spiritual protection and remove tapu.

example_sentences.maori: Me karakia tātou i mua i te kai.
example_sentences.english: Let us say a prayer before we eat.

definition: A tribe or large social grouping descended from a common ancestor. Iwi are made up of hapū (sub-tribes), which are made up of whānau (family groups). Also means 'bones'.
A sub-tribe or clan within an iwi. The hapū was the primary political and military unit in traditional Māori society. Also means pregnant.
Language, voice, or dialect. Te Reo Māori (the Māori language) is a taonga tuku iho — a treasure passed down through generations. It is an official language of Aotearoa New Zealand.

example_sentences.maori: Ko te reo Māori tōku reo.
example_sentences.english: Te Reo Māori is my language.

english: Land of the Long White Cloud
definition: The Māori name for New Zealand, traditionally translated as 'Land of the Long White Cloud'. Named by the legendary navigator Kupe's wife Kuramārōtini when she first sighted the land.

example_sentences.maori: He ataahua tō tātou whenua o Aotearoa.
example_sentences.english: Our land of Aotearoa is beautiful.

definition: Beautiful, pretty, or attractive. Can describe people, places, objects, or sounds.
School or place of learning. Also means precious or treasured. Kura kaupapa Māori are schools that teach entirely in te reo Māori.

example_sentences.maori: Ka haere ngā tamariki ki te kura.

definition: Knowledge, education, and wisdom. Mātauranga Māori refers specifically to the body of Māori knowledge — including science, philosophy, history, and cultural practice — developed over centuries.
Welcome! Come here! A call of greeting and invitation. Often repeated three times in a formal welcome: 'Haere mai, haere mai, haere mai!'

example_sentences.maori: Haere mai, haere mai, haere mai ki tō mātou marae.
example_sentences.english: Welcome, welcome, welcome to our marae.

definition: Welcome. Often paired with 'haere mai' as 'nau mai, haere mai' — a combined welcome greeting.

example_sentences.maori: Nau mai, haere mai ki Aotearoa.

definition: A term of address meaning 'friend' or 'mate'. Very commonly used in everyday speech. Hoa alone also means friend.

example_sentences.maori: Kei te pēhea koe, e hoa?

definition: Good morning. A commonly used greeting in the morning, used across Aotearoa regardless of te reo fluency.

example_sentences.english: Good morning! Did you sleep well?

definition: To show respect, care for, and support others. Manaaki is the verb form underlying manaakitanga — the act of uplifting others' mana through generous and respectful action.
The heart or inner self — the seat of feelings, emotions, and character. Ngākau describes the emotional and spiritual interior of a person.
Kindness, mercy, and compassion shown toward others. Atawhai refers to the practice of treating others with grace and generous care.
Strong; also used as a noun for strength, ability, or vigour. Ko tōku whānau tōku kaha — my family is my strength.
To sow seeds or plant; also used figuratively to instil values or feelings into others. From the whakatauki: He aroha whakatō, he aroha puta mai — if you sow love, love will be returned.
To emerge, come out, or appear. Used in puta mai (to come forth/return) and puta ake (to come up/arise). Paired with whakatō in the whakatauki about love returning.
To help, assist, or support. Commonly used in everyday speech: Kia ora mō tō āwhina — thank you for your help.
Good, fine, nice, or pleasant. One of the most common words in everyday te reo Māori. Kei te pai — it's fine / I'm good.
Yes; an affirmative particle. The opposite is kāo (no). Widely used in everyday conversation.
An intensifier meaning 'very' or 'truly'. Tino pai — very good. Tino nui — very large. Also used to mean genuine or real.
Pleasant, delicious, or sweet — of taste, sound, or experience. He reka te kai — the food is delicious.
To drink. The verb for consuming liquid. He inu wai — a drink of water.
A feast or celebratory meal. Hākari were important occasions for manaakitanga — expressing generosity and hospitality through shared food.
A traditional cooking method using heated stones in an earth pit. Food is wrapped and lowered into the hāngī, covered with earth, and slow-cooked for several hours. Also refers to the meal itself.
A lake or enclosed body of water. Also used as a locative preposition meaning 'inside' or 'within' (i roto i — inside of).
The sea or coast; salt water, as distinct from freshwater (wai māori). The tides are ngā tai. Taiāpure refers to coastal fishing areas.
Rain; also the verb 'to rain'. E ua ana — it is raining. Rain is seen as a blessing in many contexts, associated with the heavens (rangi).
A hill or elevated mound. Smaller than a maunga (mountain), puke often appear in place names across Aotearoa.
The summit or peak of a mountain or hill. Also used metaphorically for reaching the highest point of achievement.
A tree, plant, or piece of wood. Also refers to traditional medicines derived from plants (rongoā rākau). Rākau is one of the most versatile words in te reo Māori.
A path, road, or route; also the verb to arise or get up. Ara! — an exclamation of surprise. Te ara o ngā tīpuna — the pathway of the ancestors.
A star or stars. Whetū were used for navigation by Māori voyagers crossing the Pacific. Ko Matariki — the Pleiades star cluster — is the most significant constellation.

english: space in front of the wharenui
definition: The open space or forecourt of a marae, in front of the wharenui (meeting house). The ātea is a sacred space where formal proceedings and debates take place.
The Māori lunar calendar and almanac, used to determine the best times for planting, fishing, harvesting, and other activities. Based on the phases of the moon (marama) and the movement of stars.

The Earth mother in Māori cosmology, paired with Ranginui (the Sky father). All living things are her children. The bond between Papatūānuku and Ranginui was so strong that their embrace kept the world in darkness until their children separated them.
The natural world or environment. Kaitiakitanga is the responsibility to protect the taiao. The concept encompasses all living things, the land, sea, and sky.

A god, supernatural being, or powerful ancestor spirit. The principal atua include Tāne (forests), Tangaroa (sea), Rūaumoko (earthquakes), and Tūmatauenga (people). Atua are venerated through karakia.

A treasure or precious object — material or intangible. Te Reo Māori is a taonga tuku iho (treasure passed down through generations). The Treaty of Waitangi guarantees Māori the protection of their taonga.

Traditional Māori medicine and healing practices. Rongoā rākau uses plants and trees; rongoā tinana addresses physical healing; rongoā wairua addresses spiritual wellbeing. Practiced by tohunga (specialists).

english: atua of the ocean and fish
definition: The atua (god) of the sea, fish, and all ocean creatures. Tangaroa is one of the principal atua in Māori cosmology. Fishermen would call on Tangaroa before going to sea.

Man or male person. Also Tāne-mahuta, the atua of forests, birds, and human life — who shaped the first human (Hineahuone) from earth. Tāne separated his parents Ranginui and Papatūānuku to let in the light.
Right, correct, and just. Tika is one of the three core principles of Māori ethics alongside pono (truth) and aroha (love). He tika — it is right/correct.
Truth, honesty, and sincerity. Pono is one of the three core values of Māori ethics alongside tika (right) and aroha (love). Ko te pono, ko te tika, ko te aroha.
A guardian, trustee, or steward. The kaitiaki holds responsibility for protecting a resource, person, or sacred object. Kaitiaki act on behalf of their people and the natural world.

english: people with authority over the land
definition: The people who hold traditional customary authority and connection to a specific area of land. Mana whenua have genealogical ties to the land that confer rights and responsibilities.
A person or human being. Tangata whenua — people of the land (indigenous people). Tangata Māori — Māori person. The plural is tāngata.
Ancestors or grandparents. Tīpuna (also tūpuna) refers to those who came before — the link between the living and the past. Whakapapa traces connections back to tīpuna. Singular: tīpuna or tūpuna.
A son, boy, or young male. Tama is also used as a term of affection for a younger male. Tāmoko (ta moko) takes its first syllable from this root.
A daughter, girl, or young female. Tamāhine is the feminine counterpart to tama. The macron on the second syllable distinguishes it in pronunciation.
A grandchild or descendant. Mokopuna represent the future of the iwi and carry the mana and knowledge of their tīpuna. Plural: mokopuna (unchanged).

An elder or respected older person. Kaumātua hold knowledge of tikanga (customs), whakapapa, and tradition. They provide guidance and leadership in hapū and iwi. Kuia are female elders; koroua are male elders.
The carved prow or figurehead of a waka (canoe). The tauihu features intricate carvings representing the identity and whakapapa of the waka's owners. It leads the canoe through the water.
The hand, arm, or fingers. Rima means both five and hand (as a hand has five fingers); ringaringa is the more specific term for the hand and arm.
The sweet potato (Ipomoea batatas), brought to Aotearoa by Māori ancestors from Polynesia. Kūmara was a staple food and highly valued — stored in specialised storage pits called rua kūmara.
Black or dark-coloured. Used interchangeably with mangu in many dialects. Ka pango ka whero — black and red (the colours of strength and vitality in traditional Māori art).
Orange in colour. Also the name of the karaka tree (Corynocarpus laevigatus), a native tree with orange berries. The colour takes its name from these distinctive berries.
Yellow or golden in colour. Also the kōwhai tree (Sophora), Aotearoa's national flower — its golden flowers bloom in spring and are a beloved symbol of the country.

A song or chant; also the verb to sing. Waiata are an essential part of Māori culture — sung at tangi (funerals), hui (gatherings), and as accompaniment to speeches. Learning waiata is central to te reo learning.
To speak or talk; also a noun for speech, conversation, or language. Kōrerotia te reo — speak the language! Kōrero is fundamental to marae culture and the transmission of knowledge.
A story, legend, or traditional narrative. Pūrākau carry the history, values, and teachings of a people. They are told by kaumātua and tīpuna to transmit knowledge across generations.
Formal introductions or greetings, particularly on a marae. A mihimihi typically includes one's mountain, river, iwi, hapū, and name — establishing identity and connection to place.

The traditional gathering place of a hapū or iwi, centred on the ātea (open courtyard) and wharenui (meeting house). The marae is the cultural heart of the community — used for tangi, hui, celebrations, and formal ceremonies.
A classroom or room of learning. From ako (to learn/teach) and manga (a tributary). Used in school contexts.
A teacher or instructor. Kaiako hold the knowledge (mātauranga) and guide ākonga (learners). The term is used in both English-medium and Māori-medium educational settings.
A student or learner. The ākonga is the receiver of mātauranga from the kaiako. From the verb ako — to learn and to teach (acknowledging that learning is a two-way process).
To know or to understand; also used as an adjective meaning knowledgeable or wise. He mōhio nui — very knowledgeable. Often appears in contexts of traditional knowledge and expertise.
A friend or companion. E hoa is the vocative form used to address a friend directly. Hoa pūmau — lasting friend. Hoa rangatira — respected companion.

Morning or early dawn — the time before full sunrise. Also means a shadow, reflection, or image. Ata mārie — peaceful dawn (used as a greeting). He pai tō moe? — Did you sleep well? (greeting in the morning).
To sleep; also a noun for sleep or a dream. He pai tō moe? — Did you sleep well? Moe mai rā — rest in peace (said of the deceased). Moemoeā — dream.

Traditional Māori performing arts encompassing waiata (song), haka (posture dance), and other forms of cultural expression. Kapa haka groups perform at competitions, hui, and celebrations. A kapa is a row or company of performers.

A sacred or spiritually restricted place. Wāhi tapu sites include burial grounds, places of historical significance, and locations associated with atua or ancestral events. Access is governed by tapu.
A formal greeting addressed to exactly two people. From tēnā (there is/greetings to) + kōrua (you two). Tēnā koe = one person, tēnā kōrua = two, tēnā koutou = three or more.

A formal greeting addressed to three or more people. Used in formal speeches and ceremonial contexts. Often repeated for emphasis: Tēnā koutou, tēnā koutou, tēnā koutou katoa — greetings, greetings, greetings to you all.
A common response meaning 'I'm fine' or 'it's good'. Used in response to kei te pēhea koe? (how are you?). Also used to affirm that something is acceptable.
